core_behavior.note: Model B adds an explicit no-side-effects constraint and single-pass collection requirement that Models A and C do not specify.

inputs.note: Model B enumerates multiple input locations (headers, path params, query strings) and explicitly defines null/empty-string as missing; Models A and C are narrower; none of the three models address wrong-type as a missing-field trigger.

outputs.note: Model B specifies a detailed nested error.fields structure with dot-notation and location, plus a custom X-Error-Code header; Models A and C use simpler flat structures without those additions.

assumptions.note: Model A explicitly assumes only field presence is checked (not validity), Model B treats null/empty-string as missing, and Model C defers to a configuration store; none address simultaneous reporting of all missing fields as an explicit assumption.

failure_modes.note: Models A and C specify failure modes for malformed bodies and internal errors; Model B has an empty failure_modes array.

edge_cases.note: Models A and C enumerate edge cases; Model B has an empty edge_cases array; none of the models address field-present-but-wrong-type as an edge case.

security_constraints.note: Models A and C specify security constraints; Model B has an empty security_constraints array; none of the models explicitly address preventing disclosure of unexpected internal field names.

performance_constraints.note: Models A and C specify performance constraints; Model B has an empty performance_constraints array.

summary: OMI was the most frequently assigned category, appearing in four fields where Model B was silent while Models A and C provided content.
